did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a deep learning architecture combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Dynamic Routing between Capsules (CapsNet) for automatic melanoma recognition and skin lesion classification. The model was trained and evaluated on eleven image datasets to diagnose melanoma.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original experimental study proposing a new deep learning architecture (MEL-CAP) that fuses convolutional blocks with Dynamic Routing between Capsules. It details the methodology, hyper-tuning, and extensive experimental validation on eleven image datasets, reporting specific performance metrics (AUC, MCC) and statistical significance (p-values from Friedman's and Wilcoxon's tests) compared to state-of-the-art models.

title: Melanoma Recognition by Fusing Convolutional Blocks and Dynamic Routing between Capsules
brief_summary: This study proposes MEL-CAP, a novel deep learning architecture that fuses convolutional blocks with a customized Dynamic Routing between Capsules (CapsNet) to improve automatic melanoma recognition from skin images. The model was trained and evaluated on eleven publicly available image datasets sourced primarily from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) repository, including HAM10000 and PH2. The authors performed hyper-parameter tuning to ensure effective learning with limited data and validated predictions using two model-agnostic interpretation tools to assess the rationale behind the model's decisions. Extensive experimental results demonstrated that the proposed architecture significantly outperformed several state-of-the-art Convolutional Neural Network models. Notably, the model achieved 166% higher predictive performance compared to ResNet on non-dermoscopic images. The findings suggest that combining CNNs with CapsNets offers advantages in handling spatial hierarchies and improving diagnostic accuracy for melanoma.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target for diagnosis and classification in the proposed MEL-CAP architecture. The study focuses on automatic melanoma recognition from skin images.

Used as the negative class in the binary classification task (melanoma vs. normal/nevus). The paper notes that datasets are unbalanced and mentions 'imbalance ratio between the normal and melanoma classes'.
Mentioned only in the context of critiquing a previous study by Cruz et al. [19] which achieved low precision for this class. It was not a target class in the experiments of the current paper.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into melanoma or benign (nevus/normal) categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper performs automatic melanoma recognition and skin lesion classification. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma vs. normal/nevus) using a binary cross-entropy loss function. The architecture includes an output capsule layer with 2 capsules corresponding to the two classes.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Average distance between images of the same category (IntraC)
Average distance between images of different categories (InterC)
Ratio between intra-class and inter-class distances (DistR)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To average the results of the predictive performance evaluation.
To detect significant differences when multiple comparisons were needed among state-of-the-art models.
To detect significant differences with a control algorithm (the proposed MEL-CAP model) after Friedman's test.
To compare only two methods (e.g., baseline CapsNet vs. proposal) and reject the null hypothesis.
To determine the significance threshold for the non-parametric statistical tests.

hypothesis_testing.test: Friedman's test, Wilcoxon Signed-Rank test, and Hommel's post-hoc test
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There are no significant differences in performance among the compared models (for Friedman's test); the proposed model does not significantly outperform the control model (for Hommel's test); the distributions of differences between paired samples are symmetric around zero (for Wilcoxon test).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There are significant differences in performance among the compared models (for Friedman's test); the proposed model significantly outperforms the control model (for Hommel's test); the distributions of differences between paired samples are not symmetric around zero (for Wilcoxon test).
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Friedman's test rejected the null hypothesis for both optimization algorithms (p=0.126, though noted as no significant differences encountered in text, later p-values for other comparisons were significant) and for the comparison of AUC values (p=3.829 × 10^-8). Wilcoxon's test rejected the null hypothesis comparing the proposal to baseline CapsNet (p=3.346 × 10^-3) and with data augmentation (p=1.673 × 10^-3). Hommel's post-hoc test confirmed the proposal significantly outperformed state-of-the-art CNN models.

results_obtained_in_each_dataset.extraction_error: Could not parse a balanced JSON object

architecture_used.main_model: MEL-CAP (a hybrid architecture fusing Convolutional Neural Networks with Dynamic Routing between Capsules)
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning was applied to state-of-the-art CNN models for comparison; the MEL-CAP architecture itself was trained from scratch with hyper-parameter tuning (no specific pre-trained weights mentioned for MEL-CAP backbone).

architecture_details.backbone: Custom convolutional block replacing the first convolutional layer of CapsNet

training_details.optimizer: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) was selected as default after hyper-tuning; Adam and RMSprop were also tested.

classification_or_output_head.type: Capsule Layer followed by Length Layer
classification_or_output_head.description: Two capsules corresponding to the two classes (melanoma and benign/normal), with 64 features each.

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing all images to a resolution of 299 x 299 pixels with 3 channels (height=299, width=299, channels=3)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during training phase. Random rotation, flip, and crop transformations were used to augment the training data.

data_augmentation_techniques.technique: Class Balancing via Synthetic Image Generation
data_augmentation_techniques.details: Datasets were balanced by creating new images until the number of melanoma images was approximately equal to normal ones.
Applied during the testing phase. Each test image was augmented 10 times, and the final prediction was achieved using a soft-voting strategy.

conclusions: The proposed MEL-CAP architecture, which fuses convolutional blocks with Dynamic Routing between Capsules, significantly outperformed state-of-the-art Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) models in automatic melanoma recognition.
The model achieved 166% higher predictive performance compared to ResNet on non-dermoscopic images.
MEL-CAP achieved the best average performance across eleven image datasets, with statistically significant superiority confirmed by Friedman's test (p = 3.829 × 10^-8) and Hommel's post-hoc test.
The proposed model showed significant improvement over the baseline CapsNet architecture, with Wilcoxon's test rejecting the null hypothesis (p = 3.346 × 10^-3).
The architecture effectively handles spatial hierarchies and transformation invariance, addressing key limitations of standard CNNs in dermatology tasks.
Model-agnostic interpretation tools validated the model's predictions by highlighting the pixels activated during diagnosis, confirming the rationale behind its decisions.
Hyper-parameter tuning was essential for effective learning under limited training data conditions.

The authors acknowledge that more research is needed to demonstrate the potential of this neural network architecture in other areas and to design ensemble learning techniques for small and medium-sized problems.
important_remarks: The study relies on eleven publicly available datasets (primarily from ISIC) with no external independent clinical validation; generalizability to real-world clinical settings is unproven.

Datasets exhibit significant class imbalance (e.g., HAM10000 ImbR=6.024, MSK-3 ImbR=10.842), which the authors address via synthetic data augmentation, potentially introducing bias or overfitting to augmented patterns.
The MED-NODE dataset consists of low-resolution non-dermoscopic images taken with mobile phones, which may limit the model's performance on high-quality clinical dermoscopic images and vice versa.
The paper acknowledges that CapsNet research is in its early stages and that more research is needed to demonstrate its potential in other areas and for small/medium-sized problems.
The claim of '166% higher predictive performance' compared to ResNet is specific to non-dermoscopic images (MED-NODE), which may not reflect performance on standard dermoscopic datasets.
The model was trained from scratch with hyper-parameter tuning rather than using pre-trained weights for the MEL-CAP backbone, which may affect reproducibility and computational cost.
Interpretability was assessed using model-agnostic tools (SHAP and LIME), but these do not guarantee that the model relies on clinically relevant features rather than dataset-specific artifacts.
The study does not report sensitivity and specificity separately, focusing instead on AUC and MCC, which may obscure performance on the minority class (melanoma) in imbalanced datasets.
